topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp autumn air felt sharp against Maya’s cheeks, a sensation that made her skin prickle. She smoothed the edge of the acceptance letter on her desk for the tenth time, the paper seeming to vibrate under her touch. Every line, every carefully chosen word about her academic prowess, suddenly felt hollow, a poorly constructed facade. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in her stomach, tightening with every breath she took. She reread the section detailing the financial aid—the specific mention of the 'Northwood Merit Grant'—and the realization hit her like a physical blow. The name listed at the top wasn't hers. Her heart began to hammer against her ribs, a frantic rhythm against the quiet hum of the library. She pushed the papers away, the sound of the movement unnervingly loud in the silence.

[story 2]

Liam kept glancing at the notification on his phone, his thumb hovering over the screen as if afraid to touch it. A strange tension had settled over him, a taut wire pulled just before snapping. He’d been studying late, the glow of his laptop illuminating the dust motes dancing in the air, when he saw the email. It was official, devastatingly clear. The scholarship, the one he had poured months of sleepless nights into securing, was designated for someone else entirely. A cold sweat broke out across his forehead, dampening his collar. He stood up abruptly, the chair scraping harshly against the wooden floor. His voice, when he finally spoke to his roommate, was unnaturally high, strained. "Did you see this? It can't be right."

[story 3]

Evelyn traced the embossed seal on the official documentation, her fingers trembling slightly. The room seemed to shrink around her; the ambient noise of the dorm hallway faded into a dull murmur. She stared at the fine print, her vision blurring at the edges. It wasn't a clerical error; it was a deliberate misdirection, a cruel joke played on her ambition. A deep, unsettling stillness took hold of her limbs. She slowly lowered the paper, her breathing shallow and quick. "No," she whispered, the sound barely audible above the distant sound of music drifting from a neighboring room. The certainty of the deception settled in her chest, heavy and suffocating.
